title: Strong enhancement of Er3+ emission at room temperature in Si3N4 metasurfaces
authors: Fengkai Wei, Xinru Ji, Tobias J. Kippenberg, Duk-Yong Choi, Carsten Ronning
abstract: We report a significant enhancement of room-temperature photoluminescence from trivalent erbium-doped (Er3+) silicon nitride (Si3N4) metasurfaces at the telecommunication wavelength prepared via ion implantation. The metasurfaces, consisting of periodic nanocylinder arrays, are designed to support Mie-type resonances that tailor the local density of optical states. By systematically optimizing the nanocylinder radii, we achieve a photoluminescence (PL) enhancement factor of ~18 at a radius of 390 nm after thermal annealing, which is in excellent agreement with our simulations. Time-resolved PL measurements reveal a nearly ten-fold reduction in luminescence lifetime, confirming that the enhancement is primarily driven by the Purcell effect. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the PL intensity is strongly dependent on the Er3+ ion implantation depth, with a four-fold increase in emission observed from 20 nm to 80 nm ion range. These results provide a robust pathway for integrating efficient, active light sources into CMOS-compatible photonic device.
summary: Researchers have achieved a significant boost in erbium-doped silicon nitride light emission at room temperature by using metasurfaces to create Mie resonances. By optimizing the size of nanocylinders and the depth of ion implantation, they achieved an 18-fold increase in brightness. This provides a way to integrate active light sources into standard silicon-based photonic chips.
main_problem: Achieving efficient, room-temperature 1550 nm photoluminescence in silicon nitride (Si3N4) for active on-chip photonics, which is typically limited by low spontaneous emission and non-radiative recombination.
main_result: Demonstrated an 18-fold enhancement of Er3+ photoluminescence at room temperature using Mie-type resonances in Si3N4 nanocylinder metasurfaces, driven by the Purcell effect.
method: The study combines ion implantation of Er3+ ions, thermal annealing, and electron beam lithography with FDTD and SRIM simulations to optimize the local density of optical states and ion distribution.
model_or_system: The system consists of Er3+-doped Si3N4 metasurfaces made of periodic arrays of nanocylinders (height 365 nm, radius 240-465 nm) on a fused silica substrate.
key_observables: Photoluminescence (PL) intensity, luminescence lifetime, Purcell factor, and ion implantation depth/range.
important_parameters: Nanocylinder radius (optimal at 390 nm), implantation energy (50 to 350 keV), and wavelength (1530 nm/C-band).
main_assumptions: The enhancement is primarily driven by the Purcell effect via Mie-type resonances; the system operates in a regime where thermal annealing effectively passivates defects.
figures_summary: The notes describe PL spectral heatmaps showing the relationship between nanocylinder radii and implantation energies, and a plot of peak PL intensity as a function of implantation energy and ion range.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of active Si3N4 photonics, describes the fabrication and doping process, presents electromagnetic simulations and ion distribution modeling, details the experimental PL and lifetime measurements, and discusses the optimization of ion depth and resonance geometry.
why_interesting: The paper demonstrates significant control over the Purcell effect and local density of optical states (LDOS) in a CMOS-compatible platform, which is highly relevant to the development of integrated quantum optical components and light-matter interfaces.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
